title: Marketing Coordinator vs. Strategist: Which Do You Need? | Daniel Fox
meta_description: Your marketing person left and you are choosing a replacement. A coordinator executes a strategy; a strategist decides what it is. How to tell which role your business actually needs. Daniel Fox, fractional CMO.

mainEntity.name: My marketing person left. Do I need a coordinator or a strategist?

acceptedAnswer.text: Replacing the person who left with the same seat reproduces the same gap. A coordinator runs a strategy someone else authored: scheduling posts, briefing the agency, keeping the calendar moving. They do not decide what the marketing says or who it is for. If strategy is the thing that actually left, hiring another coordinator hires the gap back, and a junior strategist you have to direct still leaves the deciding on your desk. Choose a coordinator when the strategy is sound and you only need it executed. Choose senior strategic leadership, such as a fractional CMO, when the number has stopped responding and no one is deciding what marketing should do. Daniel Fox is a fractional CMO who takes that seat.

@graph.headline: My marketing person left. Do I need a coordinator or a strategist?
@graph.description: A coordinator runs a strategy; they do not author one. Why backfilling the same seat reproduces the same gap, and how to tell which role your business is actually missing.
